In [New Zealand politics](/wiki/Politics_of_New_Zealand "Politics of New Zealand"), **Māori electorates**, colloquially known as the **Māori seats** ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *ngā tūru Māori*),[[1]](#cite_note-1) are a special category of [electorate](/wiki/New_Zealand_electorates "New Zealand electorates") that give [reserved positions](/wiki/Reserved_political_positions "Reserved political positions") to representatives of [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") in the [New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament"). Every area in New Zealand is covered by both a general and a Māori electorate; as of 2020, there are seven Māori electorates.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Since 1967, [candidates](/wiki/Political_candidate "Political candidate") in Māori electorates have not needed to be Māori themselves, but to register as a [voter](/wiki/Voting_in_New_Zealand "Voting in New Zealand") in the Māori electorates people need to declare that they are of Māori descent.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Māori electorates were introduced in 1867 under the Maori Representation Act.[[5]](#cite_note-5) They were created in order to give Māori a more direct say in parliament. The [first Māori elections](/wiki/First_M%C4%81ori_elections "First Māori elections") were held in the following year during the term of the [4th New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/4th_New_Zealand_Parliament "4th New Zealand Parliament"). The electorates were intended as a temporary measure lasting five years but were extended in 1872 and made permanent in 1876.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Despite numerous attempts to dismantle Māori electorates, they continue to form a distinctive feature of the New Zealand political system.[[7]](#cite_note-Origins_of_the_Māori_Seats-7)

## Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Organisation")]

Māori electorates operate much as do general electorates, but have as electors people who are [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"), or of Māori descent, (*see [Māori people § Demographics](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people#Demographics "Māori people")*) and who choose to place their names on a separate [electoral roll](/wiki/Electoral_roll "Electoral roll") rather than on the "general roll".

There are two features of the Māori electorates that make them distinct from the general electorates. First, there are a number of skills that are essential for candidates to have in order to engage with their constituencies and ensure a clear line of accountability to representing the 'Māori voice'. This includes proficiency in *[te reo Māori](/wiki/Te_reo_M%C4%81ori "Te reo Māori")*, knowledge of *[tikanga Māori](/wiki/Tikanga_M%C4%81ori "Tikanga Māori")*, *[whakawhanaungatanga](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/whakawhanaungatanga#Māori "wikt:whakawhanaungatanga")* skills and confidence on the *[marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae")*. Second, the geographical size of the Māori electoral boundaries vary significantly from the general electorates. Five to 18 general electorates fit into any one Māori electorate.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBargh2015302–303-8)

Māori electoral boundaries are superimposed over the electoral boundaries used for general electorates; thus every part of New Zealand simultaneously belongs both in a general seat and in a Māori seat. Shortly after each census all registered Māori electors have the opportunity to choose whether they are included on the Māori or general electorate rolls.[[9]](#cite_note-Māori_Electoral_Option_2013-9) Since 31 March 2023, Māori electors have been able to change rolls at any time, except in the three months preceding a general or local election or after a notice of vacancy is issued for a by-election.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Each five-yearly census and Māori Electoral Option determines the number of Māori electorates for the next one or two elections.

## Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Establishment")]

Māori electorates were created in 1867 during the term of the [4th Parliament](/wiki/4th_New_Zealand_Parliament "4th New Zealand Parliament") with the Maori Representation Act, drafted by [Napier](/wiki/Napier_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Napier (New Zealand electorate)") member of parliament [Donald McLean](/wiki/Donald_McLean_(New_Zealand_politician) "Donald McLean (New Zealand politician)").[[7]](#cite_note-Origins_of_the_Māori_Seats-7) Parliament passed the act after lengthy debate, and during a period of warfare between the government and some North Island Māori [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū"); it was seen as a way to reduce conflict between cultures.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Its primary aim was to enfranchise Māori who were indirectly excluded from parliament by the land ownership requirement that applied in New Zealand at that time. To vote, a person had to be male, a subject of the monarch, have title to land worth at least 25 pounds, and not be in prison. Very few Māori qualified because of the [property qualification](/wiki/Property_qualification "Property qualification") — the land they owned was held in common and not by Crown grant: native title was not accepted as a way to assert a right to the franchise. Concern was raised that, indirectly, this ran contrary to section III of the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") which made all Māori subjects of the monarch with corresponding voting and representation rights.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBargh2015302–303-8)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The act originally agreed to set up four electorates specially for Māori; three in the North Island and one covering the whole South Island. The four seats were a fairly modest concession on a per-capita basis at the time.[[13]](#cite_note-Seats-13) Some MPs, such as [James FitzGerald](/wiki/James_FitzGerald_(New_Zealand_politician) "James FitzGerald (New Zealand politician)"), regarded the concessions given to Māori as insufficient, while others disagreed. In the end, the setting up of Māori electorates separate from existing electorates assuaged the conservative opposition to the bill. The bill was intended as a temporary measure, giving specific representation to Māori until the land ownership issue was resolved. However, the Māori seats continued and have become a permanent feature of the New Zealand parliament.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-Seats-13)

### Early Maori MPs elected

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Maori MPs elected")]

The first four Māori members of parliament, elected in [1868](/wiki/First_M%C4%81ori_elections "First Māori elections"), were [Tāreha Te Moananui](/wiki/T%C4%81reha_Te_Moananui "Tāreha Te Moananui") ([Eastern Maori](/wiki/Eastern_Maori "Eastern Maori")), [Frederick Nene Russell](/wiki/Frederick_Nene_Russell "Frederick Nene Russell") ([Northern Maori](/wiki/Northern_Maori "Northern Maori")) and [John Patterson](/wiki/John_Patterson_(Southern_Maori_politician) "John Patterson (Southern Maori politician)") ([Southern Maori](/wiki/Southern_Maori "Southern Maori")), who all retired in 1870; and [Mete Kīngi Te Rangi Paetahi](/wiki/Mete_K%C4%ABngi_Paetahi "Mete Kīngi Paetahi") ([Western Maori](/wiki/Western_Maori "Western Maori")) who was defeated in 1871. These four men were the first New Zealand-born members of the New Zealand Parliament.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The second four members were [Karaitiana Takamoana](/wiki/Karaitiana_Takamoana "Karaitiana Takamoana") (Eastern Maori); [Wi Katene](/wiki/Wi_Katene "Wi Katene") (Northern Maori); [Hōri Kerei Taiaroa](/wiki/H%C5%8Dri_Kerei_Taiaroa "Hōri Kerei Taiaroa") (Southern Maori); and [Wiremu Parata](/wiki/Wiremu_Parata "Wiremu Parata") (Western Maori).[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The first Māori woman MP was [Iriaka Rātana](/wiki/Iriaka_R%C4%81tana "Iriaka Rātana"), who represented the Western Maori electorate. Like [Elizabeth McCombs](/wiki/Elizabeth_McCombs "Elizabeth McCombs"), New Zealand's first woman MP, Rātana won the seat in a by-election caused by the death of her husband [Matiu](/wiki/Matiu_R%C4%81tana "Matiu Rātana") in 1949.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Elections: conduct and rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Elections: conduct and rules")]

Currently Māori elections are held as part of [New Zealand general elections](/wiki/New_Zealand_elections "New Zealand elections"), but in the past such elections took place separately, on different days (usually the day before the vote for general electorates) and under different rules. Historically, less organisation went into holding Māori elections than general elections, and the process received fewer resources. Māori electorates at first did not require registration for voting, which was later introduced. New practices such as [paper ballots](/wiki/Ballot "Ballot") (as opposed to casting one's vote verbally) and [secret ballots](/wiki/Secret_ballot "Secret ballot") also came later to elections for Māori electorates than to general electorates.

The authorities frequently delayed or overlooked reforms of the Māori electoral system, with Parliament considering the Māori electorates as largely unimportant. The gradual improvement of Māori elections owes much to long-serving Māori MP [Eruera Tirikatene](/wiki/Eruera_Tirikatene "Eruera Tirikatene"), who himself experienced problems in his own election. From the [election of 1951](/wiki/1951_New_Zealand_general_election "1951 New Zealand general election") onwards, the voting for Māori and general electorates was held on the same day.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson1985138-18)

Confusion around the Māori electorates during the 2017 general election was revealed in a number of complaints to the Electoral Commission. Complaints included Electoral Commission staff at polling booths being unaware of the Māori roll and insisting electors were unregistered when their names did not appear on the general roll; Electoral Commission staff giving incorrect information about the Māori electorates; electors being given incorrect voting forms and electors being told they were unable to vote for [Te Pāti Māori](/wiki/Te_P%C4%81ti_M%C4%81ori "Te Pāti Māori") (the Māori Party) unless they were on the Māori roll.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Switching between rolls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Switching between rolls")]

In June 2022, the [Justice Minister](/wiki/Minister_of_Justice_(New_Zealand) "Minister of Justice (New Zealand)") [Kris Faafoi](/wiki/Kris_Faafoi "Kris Faafoi") of the incumbent [Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") introduced a bill to allow people of Māori descent to switch between the general and Māori electoral rolls at any time. At the time, Māori were only allowed to switch between the two rolls every five years. To pass into law, the bill needed 75% majority support in Parliament. In addition, Māori Party co-leader [Rawiri Waititi](/wiki/Rawiri_Waititi "Rawiri Waititi") introduced a member's bill which proposed automatically placing Māori on the Māori electoral roll and renaming the "general electoral district" the "non-Māori electoral district."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On 15 November 2022, the opposition [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") abandoned its opposition to the Māori Electoral Option bill after the [Labour Government](/wiki/Sixth_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand "Sixth Labour Government of New Zealand") agreed to allow people of Māori descent to switch between the general and Māori rolls at any time except the three-month period before general and local elections; giving the Government the 75% majority need to pass the bill into law. Te Pāti Māori criticised the compromise, with Waititi and fellow co-leader [Debbie Ngarewa-Packer](/wiki/Debbie_Ngarewa-Packer "Debbie Ngarewa-Packer") describing the changes as "second-rate" and a "half pie ka pai" respectively. Waititi's member bill had already been voted down in early November.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The [Electoral (Māori Electoral Option) Legislation Act 2022](/wiki/Electoral_(M%C4%81ori_Electoral_Option)_Legislation_Act_2022 "Electoral (Māori Electoral Option) Legislation Act 2022") came into force on 31 March 2023; allowing people of Māori descent to switch between the general and Māori rolls at anytime until the three-month period before elections. The [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(New_Zealand) "Electoral Commission (New Zealand)") subsequently launched a campaign to encourage non-voters to register with either the general or Māori rolls.[[22]](#cite_note-22) For the [2023 New Zealand general election](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election"), the cutoff date was set at midnight 13 July 2023.[[23]](#cite_note-23) By 3 July 2023, over 12,000 people had switched between the Māori and general rolls; with 6,662 people shifting from the general to Māori rolls and 5,652 switching vice versa.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Political expert and academic Rawiri Taonui and journalist Tommy de Silva described that the increase of voters on the Māori roll as a form of strategic voting that reinforced the relevance of the Māori seats and Māori vote to New Zealand politics.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

By 11 July 2025, the Electoral Commission confirmed that almost 31,000 voters had switched to the Māori electoral roll while over 25,000 first time voters had registered with the Māori roll. By contrast, about 20,000 Māori descent voters had switched to the general roll while 17,000 new Māori voters had registered with the general roll. Commission chief Māori advisor Hone Matthews credited the 2022 law change with removing a barrier for Māori voters and boosting their electoral engagement.[[27]](#cite_note-27) On 31 July, Te Pāti Māori filed urgent [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_New_Zealand "High Court of New Zealand") proceedings calling on the Electoral Commission, [Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(New_Zealand) "Ministry of Justice (New Zealand)") and the Ombudsman to investigate reports that Māori voters had been removed from the Māori electoral roll or shifted to the general roll without their consent.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Calls for abolition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Calls for abolition")]

There have been calls for the abolition of the Māori electorates.

### Mid-20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mid-20th century")]

In 1953, re-alignment of Māori electoral boundaries occurred. In the 1950s the practice of reserving electorates for Māori was described by some politicians "as a form of '[apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid")', like in South Africa".[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 1967, the electoral system whereby four electorate seats were reserved for representatives who were specifically Māori ended. Following the Electoral Amendment Act 1967, the 100-year-old disqualification preventing Europeans from standing as candidates in Māori electorates was removed. Simultaneously, the act allowed Māori to stand in general electorates.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1976, the [National Government](/wiki/Third_National_Government_of_New_Zealand "Third National Government of New Zealand") introduced the option for Māori to decide whether to enrol individually on the general electoral roll or the Māori roll.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie1989119-31) A large number of people (Māori and non-Māori) failed to fill out an electoral re-registration card that was distributed with the [1976 census](/wiki/1976_New_Zealand_census "1976 New Zealand census"), with census staff lacking authority to insist on the card being completed. This had little practical effect for non-Māori, but it transferred Māori to the general roll if the card was not handed in.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcRobie1989119-31)

### Current positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Current positions")]

[Hobson's Pledge](/wiki/Hobson%27s_Pledge "Hobson's Pledge"), a lobby group founded by [Don Brash](/wiki/Don_Brash "Don Brash"), a former senior politician, advocates abolishing the allocated Māori electorates, seeing them as outdated.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Other parties' positions on abolition or reform are detailed below.

#### National Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: National Party")]

The [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") has advocated abolition of the Māori electorates, though as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit) the party is not opposed to the seats. National did not stand candidates in Māori electorate from the 2005 election through the 2020 election. [Bill English](/wiki/Bill_English "Bill English"), the party's leader in 2003, said that "the purpose of the Māori seats has come to an end", and in 2004 party leader [Don Brash](/wiki/Don_Brash "Don Brash") called the electorates an "anachronism".[[33]](#cite_note-:0-33) National announced in 2008 it would abolish the electorates when all historic [treaty settlements](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi_claims_and_settlements "Treaty of Waitangi claims and settlements") have been resolved, which it aimed to complete by 2014.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In 2014 though, then-Prime Minister [John Key](/wiki/John_Key "John Key") ruled out the abolition, saying he would not do it even if he had the numbers to do so as there would be "[hikois](/wiki/H%C4%ABkoi "Hīkoi") from hell".[[35]](#cite_note-35) In 2020, party leader [Judith Collins](/wiki/Judith_Collins "Judith Collins") said "I am not opposed to the Māori seats. The National Party has had a view for many years now that they should be done away with. But I just want people to feel that they all have opportunities for representation".[[36]](#cite_note-36)

#### ACT Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: ACT Party")]

The [ACT Party](/wiki/ACT_New_Zealand "ACT New Zealand") opposes the Māori electorates. Its leader, [David Seymour](/wiki/David_Seymour "David Seymour"), has called for their abolition as recently as 2019.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

#### New Zealand First

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: New Zealand First")]

[New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First") whose "[Tight Five](/wiki/Tight_Five "Tight Five")" once held all Māori seats, has advocated for abolition of the separate electorates, while emphasising that the decision should be made by Māori voters. During the [2017 election campaign](/wiki/2017_New_Zealand_general_election "2017 New Zealand general election"), the New Zealand First leader [Winston Peters](/wiki/Winston_Peters "Winston Peters") announced that if elected his party would hold a binding referendum on whether Maori electorates should be abolished.[[38]](#cite_note-38) During post-election negotiations with the Labour Party, Peters indicated that he would consider dropping his call for a referendum on the Māori electorates due to the defeat of the [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party") at the 2017 election.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In return for forming a government with the [Labour Party](/wiki/Sixth_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand "Sixth Labour Government of New Zealand"), New Zealand First agreed to drop its demand for the referendum.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 2026 the party returned to campaigning on a referendum to abolish the seats, arguing they were racially decisive and no longer needed as a higher proportion of MPs being Māori (27%), and four of the six parliamentary parties had Māori leaders.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The party has not stood candidates in the Māori electorates since the [1999 New Zealand general election](/wiki/1999_New_Zealand_general_election "1999 New Zealand general election").

## Individual electorates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Individual electorates")]

From 1868 to 1996, four Māori electorates existed (out of a total that slowly changed from 76 to 99).[[43]](#cite_note-General_elections_1853-2005-43) They comprised:[[44]](#cite_note-Scholefield-44)

1. [Eastern Maori](/wiki/Eastern_Maori "Eastern Maori")
2. [Northern Maori](/wiki/Northern_Maori "Northern Maori")
3. [Southern Maori](/wiki/Southern_Maori "Southern Maori")
4. [Western Maori](/wiki/Western_Maori "Western Maori")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/1996_New_Zealand_general_election.svg/250px-1996_New_Zealand_general_election.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1996_New_Zealand_general_election.svg)

Electorates in the 1996 election (Māori seats shown bottom-right)

With the introduction of the [MMP](/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional_representation "Mixed-member proportional representation") electoral system after 1993, the rules regarding the Māori electorates changed. Today, the number of electorates floats, meaning that the electoral population of a Māori seat can remain roughly equivalent to that of a general seat. For the [1996 election](/wiki/1996_New_Zealand_general_election "1996 New Zealand general election"), the first under MMP, the Electoral Commission defined five Māori electorates:

1. [Te Puku O Te Whenua](/wiki/Te_Puku_O_Te_Whenua "Te Puku O Te Whenua") (*The belly of the land*)
2. [Te Tai Hauauru](/wiki/Te_Tai_Hauauru "Te Tai Hauauru") (*The western district*)
3. [Te Tai Rawhiti](/wiki/Te_Tai_Rawhiti "Te Tai Rawhiti") (*The eastern district*)
4. [Te Tai Tokerau](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau "Te Tai Tokerau") (*The northern district*)
5. [Te Tai Tonga](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tonga "Te Tai Tonga") (*The southern district*)

Major changes were made and a sixth Māori electorate was added for the second MMP election in [1999](/wiki/1999_New_Zealand_general_election "1999 New Zealand general election"):

1. [Hauraki](/wiki/Hauraki_(M%C4%81ori_electorate) "Hauraki (Māori electorate)")
2. [Ikaroa-Rawhiti](/wiki/Ikaroa-R%C4%81whiti "Ikaroa-Rāwhiti")
3. [Te Tai Hauāuru](/wiki/Te_Tai_Hau%C4%81uru "Te Tai Hauāuru")
4. [Te Tai Tokerau](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau "Te Tai Tokerau")
5. [Te Tai Tonga](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tonga "Te Tai Tonga")
6. [Waiariki](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)")

Since [2002](/wiki/2002_New_Zealand_general_election "2002 New Zealand general election"), there have been seven Māori electorates. For the 2002 and [2005 elections](/wiki/2005_New_Zealand_general_election "2005 New Zealand general election"), these were:

1. [Ikaroa-Rāwhiti](/wiki/Ikaroa-R%C4%81whiti "Ikaroa-Rāwhiti")
2. [Tainui](/wiki/Tainui_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tainui (New Zealand electorate)")
3. [Tāmaki Makaurau](/wiki/T%C4%81maki_Makaurau_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tāmaki Makaurau (New Zealand electorate)") (roughly equivalent to greater [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"))
4. [Te Tai Hauāuru](/wiki/Te_Tai_Hau%C4%81uru "Te Tai Hauāuru")
5. [Te Tai Tokerau](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau "Te Tai Tokerau")
6. [Te Tai Tonga](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tonga "Te Tai Tonga")
7. [Waiariki](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)")

From [2008](/wiki/2008_New_Zealand_general_election "2008 New Zealand general election"), Tainui was largely replaced by Hauraki-Waikato, giving the following seven Māori electorates:

1. [Hauraki-Waikato](/wiki/Hauraki-Waikato "Hauraki-Waikato") – north-western [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island"), including [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand") and [Papakura](/wiki/Papakura_District "Papakura District")
2. [Ikaroa-Rāwhiti](/wiki/Ikaroa-R%C4%81whiti "Ikaroa-Rāwhiti") – eastern and southern North Island, including [Gisborne](/wiki/Gisborne,_New_Zealand "Gisborne, New Zealand") and [Masterton](/wiki/Masterton "Masterton")
3. [Tāmaki Makaurau](/wiki/T%C4%81maki_Makaurau_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tāmaki Makaurau (New Zealand electorate)") – southern and central Auckland, and parts of western Auckland
4. [Te Tai Hauāuru](/wiki/Te_Tai_Hau%C4%81uru "Te Tai Hauāuru") – western North Island, including [Taranaki](/wiki/Taranaki "Taranaki") and [Manawatū-Whanganui](/wiki/Manawat%C5%AB-Whanganui "Manawatū-Whanganui") regions
5. [Te Tai Tokerau](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau "Te Tai Tokerau") – northernmost seat, including [Whangārei](/wiki/Whang%C4%81rei "Whangārei"), northern Auckland and parts of western Auckland
6. [Te Tai Tonga](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tonga "Te Tai Tonga") – all of the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island"), [Stewart Island](/wiki/Stewart_Island "Stewart Island"), [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands"), and most of [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington"). Largest electorate by area.
7. [Waiariki](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)") – includes [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga"), [Whakatāne](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne "Whakatāne"), [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua"), [Taupō](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D "Taupō")

While seven out of 72 (9.7%) does not nearly reflect the proportion of voting-age New Zealanders who identify as being of Māori descent (about 14.8%), many Māori choose to enrol in general electorates, so the proportion reflects the proportion of voters on the Māori roll.

For maps showing broad electoral boundaries, see selected links to individual elections at [New Zealand elections](/wiki/New_Zealand_elections "New Zealand elections").

Former [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party") co-leader [Pita Sharples](/wiki/Pita_Sharples "Pita Sharples") proposed in 2007 the creation of an additional Maori electorate, for [Māori living in Australia](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_in_Australia "Māori in Australia"); there are between 115,000 and 125,000 Māori in Australia, the majority living in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland").[[45]](#cite_note-TVNZ_1385989-45)

## Party politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Party politics")]

[![Map of New Zealand with electorates superimposed, distinguishing between the six TPM seats and one Labour seat.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/M%C4%81ori_electorates_2023_general_election.png/330px-M%C4%81ori_electorates_2023_general_election.png)](/wiki/File:M%C4%81ori_electorates_2023_general_election.png)

Party representation in the Māori seats following the most recent general election, in 2023

As Māori electorates originated before the development of [political parties in New Zealand](/wiki/Political_parties_in_New_Zealand "Political parties in New Zealand"), all early Māori MPs functioned as [independents](/wiki/Independent_(politics) "Independent (politics)"). When the [Liberal Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Liberal_Party "New Zealand Liberal Party") formed, however, Māori MPs began to align themselves with the new organisation, with either Liberal candidates or Liberal sympathisers as representatives. Māori MPs in the Liberal Party included [James Carroll](/wiki/James_Carroll_(New_Zealand_politician) "James Carroll (New Zealand politician)"), [Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata") and [Te Rangi Hīroa](/wiki/Te_Rangi_H%C4%ABroa "Te Rangi Hīroa"). There were also Māori MPs in the more conservative and rural [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_(New_Zealand) "Reform Party (New Zealand)"); [Māui Pōm](/wiki/M%C4%81ui_P%C5%8Dmare "Māui Pōmare"), [Taurekareka Hēnare](/wiki/Taurekareka_H%C4%93nare "Taurekareka Hēnare") and [Taite Te Tomo](/wiki/Taite_Te_Tomo "Taite Te Tomo").

Since the [Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") first came to power in [1935](/wiki/1935_New_Zealand_general_election "1935 New Zealand general election"), however, it has dominated the Māori electorates. For a long period this dominance owed much to Labour's alliance with the [Rātana Church](/wiki/R%C4%81tana "Rātana"), although the Rātana influence has diminished in recent times. In the [1993 election](/wiki/1993_New_Zealand_general_election "1993 New Zealand general election"), however, the new [New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First") party, led by [Winston Peters](/wiki/Winston_Peters "Winston Peters") – who himself held the general seat of [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tauranga (New Zealand electorate)") from 1984 to 2005 – gained the Northern Māori seat (electing [Tau Henare](/wiki/Tau_Henare "Tau Henare") to Parliament), and in the [1996 election](/wiki/1996_New_Zealand_general_election "1996 New Zealand general election") New Zealand First captured all the Māori electorates for one electoral term. Labour regained the electorates in the following election in the [1999 election](/wiki/1999_New_Zealand_general_election "1999 New Zealand general election").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBargh2015302–303-8)

A development of particular interest to Māori came in 2004 with the resignation of [Tariana Turia](/wiki/Tariana_Turia "Tariana Turia") from her ministerial position in the Labour-dominated coalition and from her [Te Tai Hauāuru](/wiki/Te_Tai_Hau%C4%81uru "Te Tai Hauāuru") parliamentary seat. In [the resulting by-election](/wiki/2004_Te_Tai_Hauauru_by-election "2004 Te Tai Hauauru by-election") on 10 July 2004, standing under the banner of the newly formed [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party"), she received over 90% of the 7,000-plus votes cast. The parties then represented in Parliament had not put up official candidates in the by-election. The new party's support in relation to Labour therefore remained untested at the polling booth.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBargh2015305–306-46)

The Māori Party aimed to win all seven Māori electorates in [2005](/wiki/2005_New_Zealand_general_election "2005 New Zealand general election"). A [Marae-Digipoll](/w/index.php?title=Marae-Digipoll&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marae-Digipoll (page does not exist)") survey of Māori-roll voters in November 2004 gave it hope: 35.7% said they would vote for a Māori Party candidate, 26.3% opted for Labour, and five of the seven electorates appeared ready to fall to the new party.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In the election, the new party won four of the Māori electorates. It seemed possible that Māori Party MPs could play a role in the choice and formation of a governing coalition, and they conducted talks with the [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party"). In the end they remained in Opposition.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGodfery2015243–244-48)

Similarly in [2008](/wiki/2008_New_Zealand_general_election "2008 New Zealand general election"), the Māori Party aimed to win all seven Māori electorates. However, in the election, they managed to increase their four electorates only to five. Although the National government had enough MPs to govern without the Māori Party, it invited the Māori Party to support their minority government on confidence and supply in return for policy concessions and two ministerial posts outside of Cabinet. The Māori Party signed a confidence and supply agreement with National on the condition that the Māori electorates were not abolished unless the Māori voters agreed to abolish them. Other policy concessions including a review of the [Foreshore and Seabed Act 2004](/wiki/Foreshore_and_Seabed_Act_2004 "Foreshore and Seabed Act 2004"), a review of New Zealand's constitutional arrangements, and the introduction of the [Whānau Ora](/wiki/Wh%C4%81nau_Ora "Whānau Ora") indigenous health initiative.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGodfery2015244–245-49)

Discontentment with the Māori Party's support agreement with National particularly the [Marine and Coastal Areas Bill 2011](/wiki/Marine_and_Coastal_Area_(Takutai_Moana)_Act_2011 "Marine and Coastal Area (Takutai Moana) Act 2011") led the party's [Te Tai Tokerau](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau "Te Tai Tokerau") Member [Hone Harawira](/wiki/Hone_Harawira "Hone Harawira") to secede from the Māori Party and form the radical left-wing [Mana Movement](/wiki/Mana_Movement "Mana Movement"). During the [2011 general election](/wiki/2011_New_Zealand_general_election "2011 New Zealand general election"), the Māori Party retained three of the Māori electorates while Labour increased its share of the Māori electorates to three, taking [Te Tai Tonga](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tonga "Te Tai Tonga"). The Mana Movement retained Te Tai Tokerau. Tensions between the Māori Party and Mana Movement combined with competition from the Labour Party fragmented the Māori political voice in Parliament.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGodfery2015245–248-50)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBargh2015305-51)

In the [2014 election](/wiki/2014_New_Zealand_general_election "2014 New Zealand general election"), Mana Movement leader Hone Harawira formed an electoral pact with the [Internet Party](/wiki/Internet_Party_(New_Zealand) "Internet Party (New Zealand)"), founded by controversial Internet entrepreneur [Kim Dotcom](/wiki/Kim_Dotcom "Kim Dotcom") and led by former [Alliance](/wiki/Alliance_(New_Zealand_political_party) "Alliance (New Zealand political party)") MP [Laila Harré](/wiki/Laila_Harr%C3%A9 "Laila Harré") known as [Internet MANA](/wiki/Internet_Party_and_Mana_Movement "Internet Party and Mana Movement"). Hone was defeated by Labour candidate [Kelvin Davis](/wiki/Kelvin_Davis_(politician) "Kelvin Davis (politician)"), who was tacitly endorsed by the ruling National Party, New Zealand First, and the Māori Party.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Ousted_Harawira-55) During the 2014 election, Labour captured six of the Māori electorates with the Māori Party being reduced to co-leader [Te Ururoa Flavell](/wiki/Te_Ururoa_Flavell "Te Ururoa Flavell")'s [Waiariki electorate](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGodfery2015249-56) The Māori Party managed to bring a second member co-leader [Marama Fox](/wiki/Marama_Fox "Marama Fox") into Parliament as their [party vote](/wiki/Party_vote "Party vote") entitled them to one further list seat.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

During the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_New_Zealand_general_election "2017 New Zealand general election"), the Māori Party formed an electoral pact with the [Mana Movement](/wiki/Mana_Movement "Mana Movement") leader and former Māori Party MP [Hone Harawira](/wiki/Hone_Harawira "Hone Harawira") not to contest [Te Tai Tokerau](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau "Te Tai Tokerau") as part of a deal to regain the Māori electorates from the Labour Party.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Despite these efforts, Labour captured all seven of the Māori electorates with Labour candidate [Tāmati Coffey](/wiki/T%C4%81mati_Coffey "Tāmati Coffey") unseating Māori Party co-leader Flavell in Waiariki.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

[Three years later](/wiki/2020_New_Zealand_general_election "2020 New Zealand general election"), despite a historic landslide to the Labour party, Māori party candidate [Rawiri Waititi](/wiki/Rawiri_Waititi "Rawiri Waititi") successfully unseated Coffey, returning the Māori Party – now calling itself Te Pāti Māori – to Parliament. [Special votes](/wiki/Special_vote "Special vote") raised Te Pāti Māori's party vote from a provisional result of 1%[[60]](#cite_note-60) to a final party vote of 1.2%, thus allowing co-leader, [Debbie Ngarewa-Packer](/wiki/Debbie_Ngarewa-Packer "Debbie Ngarewa-Packer"), to enter Parliament as a List MP.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Following the [2023 general election](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election"), Te Pāti Māori won a record six of the seven Māori electorates, unseating Labour from all but one of the seats.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## Influence outside New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Influence outside New Zealand")]

The scheme has inspired some policymakers as a potential solution for underrepresented [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_electorates&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Australia")]

See also: [Indigenous Voice to Parliament](/wiki/Indigenous_Voice_to_Parliament "Indigenous Voice to Parliament")

In 1995, MLC [Franca Arena](/wiki/Franca_Arena "Franca Arena") moved the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales") to an inquiry and report on the idea of providing seats dedicated to people of Aboriginal background, modelled on the Māori electorates, to improve the representation of [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") in that parliament.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The Standing Committee on Social Issues, of which she was not part, released a report on the merits of the system in November 1998.[[65]](#cite_note-auto-65) The report is said to have been well-researched, with a thorough discussion of the system's mechanics, and through which paths it could come to fruition.[[66]](#cite_note-iorns-66) The NSW Government members, however, did not conclude the proposal appropriate and leaned towards other measures to facilitate Aboriginal representation.[[65]](#cite_note-auto-65)

Another report was released in 2003 by the [Legislative Assembly of Queensland](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Queensland "Legislative Assembly of Queensland")'s Legal, Constitutional and Administrative Review Committee,[[66]](#cite_note-iorns-66) as part of an inquiry on how to improve indigenous [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination"). The idea of dedicated seats in the State Parliament, while deemed to have potential to assist reconciliation efforts, was not recommended in the final report due to strong opposition from some members of the committee.[[66]](#cite_note-iorns-66)
